Temu and Shein Continue to Eat Into Amazon's U.S. Market Share, New Data Shows

By Lisa Wang | February 28, 2025 | Wall Street Journal

New data from research firm Similarweb shows that Chinese e-commerce platforms Temu and Shein have captured a combined 7.2% of U.S. online retail spending in Q4 2024, up from 4.1% a year ago. The growth has come primarily at the expense of Amazon, whose share dipped to 37.8% from 39.1%.

"Amazon's core retail business is facing its most significant competitive threat in a decade," said Brian Nowak, analyst at Morgan Stanley. "The de minimis tariff loophole has allowed Temu and Shein to undercut Amazon on price by 30-50% on commodity goods."

Amazon's third-party marketplace sellers report significant pricing pressure, with average selling prices declining 8-12% in categories where Temu competes directly, including home goods, electronics accessories, and apparel.

However, Amazon's Prime subscription model provides a significant moat. Prime member spending actually increased 6% year-over-year, while non-Prime customer spending declined 4%. Amazon's same-day and next-day delivery capabilities remain unmatched.

Amazon CFO Brian Olsavsky addressed the competition on the Q4 earnings call: "We've always competed on selection, convenience, and price. We are investing in our supply chain to further reduce delivery times, and our advertising business gives us margin flexibility that pure-play competitors don't have."

The U.S. Commerce Department is currently reviewing the de minimis exemption that allows packages under $800 to enter the country duty-free, which could significantly impact Temu and Shein's business model if revoked. A decision is expected by mid-2025.
